# Chapter 2. The Role of Capital in the Formation of Value

```{=html}
<p class="fst">In the 1st chapter of the 1st section we became acquainted with the distinction which Marx first made, that of the twofold character of commodity-producing labour: on the one hand as a <em>definite form of useful labour producing use-values</em>, and on the other hand as <em>general human simple average labour, which forms commodity values.</em> Corresponding to this twofold character, the process of production under the dominion of commodity production is likewise a twofold one; it is a unity of <em>labour process</em> and <em>value-forming process</em>, and as a <em>capitalist</em> process of production a unity of <em>labour process</em> and <em>value-expansion process.</em> In the last chapter we became acquainted with the two elements of the labour process: means of production and labour-power; but we also became acquainted with the different roles which these two elements, as parts of capital, play in the value-expansion process. We have seen that the means of production take part in the formation of the value of the product in a quite different manner than labour-power.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We have found that the value of the consumed means of production reappears in the value of the product. The transference of this value takes place in the labour process through labour. But how is this possible? Labour must simultaneously accomplish two things: create new value and transfer old value. This is only explicable through the twofold character of labour, of which we have just been reminded. In its character as <em>value-forming</em> general human labour it creates new value; in its character as a <em>particular form of useful labour producing use-values</em> it transfers the value of the means of production to the product.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only through the particular form of spinning labour can the value of cotton and spindle be transferred to the yarn; the spinner, on the other hand, can also create the same value which he creates as a spinner through other labour, if for example he becomes a joiner; but then he makes no yarn, transfers no cotton value to yarn.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The twofold character of labour as <em>value-forming</em> and <em>value-transferring</em> labour becomes clearly evident when one considers the influence of a change in the productivity of labour upon the formation of value and the transference of value. The magnitude of value which is produced in one hour of labour does not change if, other circumstances remaining equal, the productivity of labour increases or decreases. The quantity of use-values produced in a definite period of time, on the other hand, increases or decreases with the productivity of labour. To the same degree, therefore, the value-transferring capacity of labour increases or diminishes.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us assume, for example, that an invention doubles the productivity of spinning labour, while the productivity of the labour of the cotton planters remains the same. Let 1 pound of cotton contain 2 hours of labour; it costs, if we keep to our above assumption, 1 mark. Formerly 2 pounds of cotton were spun in one hour, now 4 pounds. <em>The same new value</em> which was formerly added to the 2 pounds by the labour of one hour is now added to the 4 pounds, according to our assumption 50 pfennigs. But the <em>double value</em> is now <em>transferred</em> in one hour to the yarn by the spinning labour: formerly 2 marks, now 4 marks.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>One sees that the value-<em>preserving</em> or -<em>transferring</em> power of labour rests upon a different property of it than its value-<em>forming</em> power.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Since no producing is possible without means of production, every commodity-producing labour is not only value-<em>forming</em> but also value-<em>preserving</em>, and indeed not only in the sense that it transfers the values of the consumed means of production to the product, but also in the sense that it preserves the value of the former from destruction. All earthly things are transitory, and so the means of production too perish sooner or later, even if they remain unused. Many of them, for example various machines, even deteriorate more quickly if they stand still than if they are kept in operation. With the use-value of the means of production their commodity value also vanishes. If the wear and tear takes place in the normal manner in the production process, then the value which the means of production has lost reappears in the value of the product. If the means of production wears out without being employed in the production process, then its value disappears never to return. The capitalist usually overlooks this side of labour, but it comes very keenly to his consciousness when he is compelled, perhaps as a consequence of a crisis, to interrupt the production process. Marx cites the example of an English cotton spinner who in 1862 estimated the annual costs of the standstill of his factory as a result of the cotton crisis at 120,000 marks, among them 24,000 marks for the deterioration of the machinery.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The various means of production, however, behave differently with respect to the manner of the transference of value. Some lose their independent shape in the labour process, thus raw material and auxiliary substances. Others preserve their shape in the labour process. The cotton which is spun loses its shape, the spindle which spins, however, does not. The former give up their <em>whole value</em> to the product in each production process, the latter only <em>fractional parts</em> of it. If a machine is worth 1000 marks and wears out under normal conditions in 1000 days, then in each working day it gives up the value of one mark to the product manufactured with its help during this time.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Here too the twofold character of the production process confronts us. How can the machine give up <sup><small>1</small></sup>/<small><small>1000</small></small> of its value to a definite product? For in its manufacture not <sup><small>1</small></sup>/<small><small>1000</small></small> of the machine, but the whole machine, is in operation. This objection has actually been raised. The answer is that the <em>whole</em> machine enters into the production process insofar as it is a <em>labour process</em>; whereas only a corresponding <em>fractional part</em> of it does insofar as it is a <em>value-expansion process.</em> As <em>use-value</em> the <em>whole</em> machine enters into each production process; as <em>value</em> only a <em>fractional part</em> of it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Conversely, the <em>whole value</em> of a means of production can pass over into the product, and yet only a part of its body. Let us assume that in order to produce 100 pounds of yarn 115 pounds of cotton are required under normal conditions, that the quantity of unusable waste amounts in this case to 15 pounds; then only 100 pounds of cotton will enter into the 100 pounds of yarn, but into the value of the 100 pounds of yarn the value of 115 pounds of cotton will have passed over.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The means of production transfer during the labour process as much value to the product as they themselves lose during it. They can never add more value to it than they themselves possess, however great their use-value may be. It is therefore entirely untenable when vulgar economics wishes to derive surplus value and its transformed forms—interest, profit, ground rent—from the <em>use-value</em> of the means of production, from their “services.”</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The value of the means of production consumed in the production process reappears unchanged in the value of the product.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But labour does not only preserve value, it also forms new value. Up to a certain point in time, the labour that creates new value merely replaces the value expended by the capitalist in the purchase of the labour-power. If the labour continues beyond this point, it forms excess value, <em>surplus value.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">“That part of capital, then,” says Marx, “which is converted into <em>means of production,</em> that is, into raw material, auxiliary materials and the instruments of labour, <em>does not alter the magnitude of its value</em> in the production process. I therefore call it the <em>constant</em> (unchanging, permanent) <em>part of capital,</em> or, more briefly: <em>constant capital.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quote">“The part of capital converted into <em>labour-power,</em> on the other hand, <em>does alter</em> its value in the production process. It produces its own equivalent and a surplus over and above it, <em>surplus value,</em> which may itself vary, may be greater or smaller. From a constant magnitude this part of capital is continually transformed into a variable one (one that changes in magnitude). I therefore call it the <em>variable part of capital,</em> or, more briefly: <em>variable capital.</em> The same components of capital that, from the standpoint of the labour process, are distinguished as objective and subjective factors, as means of production and labour-power, are distinguished, from the standpoint of the valorisation process, as constant capital and variable capital.”</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The magnitude of the value of constant capital is of course to be regarded as a constant magnitude only in relation to the valorisation process. The magnitude of the value of constant capital is not altered by the <em>production process</em> in which it is applied, but this may well be brought about by other factors. The ratio between constant and variable capital may also change. We shall return to this later.</p>
```


---

[↑ Previous: Chapter 1. The Process of Production](ab2-kap01.qmd) [↑ Return to Table of Contents](../index.qmd) [Next: Chapter 3. The Degree of Exploitation of Labour-Power ↓](ab2-kap03.qmd)
